content:
I'm new to Rust and I'm having trouble with something that seems pretty basic but I just can't figure it out.

How do I use KBox::new_uninit() to allocate an object of type T? I thought this would work:

pub struct DebugfsBlobEntry {
    pub dentry: *mut crate::bindings::dentry,
    pub blob: bindings::debugfs_blob_wrapper, // Add the blob wrapper
}

let mut blob  = DebugfsBlobEntry { KBox::new_uninit(GFP_KERNEL)? };

But I get this error:

79 |         let mut blob  = DebugfsBlobEntry { KBox::new_uninit(GFP_KERNEL)? };
   |                         ----------------   ----^^ expected one of `,`, `:`, or `}`
   |                         |                  |
   |                         |                  while parsing this struct field
   |                         while parsing this struct

error[E0063]: missing fields `blob` and `dentry` in initializer of `DebugfsBlobEntry`

These seems ironic, because the whole point of new_uninit is to avoid initializing anything. So what am I doing wrong?
DebugfsBlobEntry doesn't have a field of type KBox, so I'm a bit confused.
I'm trying to create a new DebugfsBlobEntry object, but I can't pre-initialize it before creating it, so I can't use ::new()
Also, why would it need to have a field of type KBox? What would that look like?
So you want a pointer to an uninitialized DebugfsBlobEntry on the heap?
If you write DebugfsBlobEntry { ... }, then you're creating an initialized one with ... specifying the fields.
Ah, I remember seeing that syntax now, thank you.
That seems to work. So since blob is of type Box<MaybeUninit<DebugfsBlobEntry>>, how do I initialize it now? blob.data is not recognized.

You can use write or write_init.

let my_init_box = my_uninit_box.write(DebugfsBloblEntry { fields... });

So the problem is that I cannot initialize all fields at once. The DebugfsBlobEntry.dentry is intialized via a call to debugfs_create_blob(), but that API requires the address of DebugfsBlobEntry.blob.
I guess I could initialize it to core::ptr::null_mut() first, and then overwrite it?
You could. Or you can keep the fields around in local variables and move them once you're ready.
ok, let me try to get that working. Thanks.

Ok, I finally got something that compiles. I didn't need to use new_uninit() after all. In a sense, I went full circle. https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=stable&mode=debug&edition=2024&gist=20d4c202c2a1a9a7463dbf0e87c0ff93
